coredata.dc:title: Nanostructured rhodamine B/aluminosilicate extracted sugarcane bagasse modified with tobacco-derived carbon quantum dot as ratiometric fluorescence probe for determination of tetracycline
coredata.dc:creator: Amiripour, Fatemeh
Ghasemi, Shahram
Chaichi, Mohammad Javad
coredata.dc:description: Tetracycline (TC), as a widely used antibiotic, is very useful in treating bacterial infections. However, its residues in animal foodstuffs can enter the human body through the food cycle and causes severe and chronic diseases. On the other hand, due to its weak non-biodegradability, it is considered a threat to the environment. In this regard, the development of sensing methods to detect and measure TC is need of the hour. Herein, a dual-emission fluorescence sensor based on porous aluminosilicate structure (ASS) with rough surface hexagonal shape morphology and pore diameter less than 2 nm was prepared. The porous AAS was modified by post-modification method with blue carbon dots (CDT) and rhodamine B (RB) as two fluorophores to develop the ratiometric fluorescence (RF) sensor (CDT-AAS/RB). Nanostructured CDT-AAS/RB emitted two resolved peaks at 445 and 585 nm , which were dramatically quenched in the presence of TC. The RF sensor, with excellent sensitivity, was able to measure TC over the linear range of 0.001–150 μM with a limit of detection of 5.4 nM in the aqueous phosphate buffer. Moreover, the AAS component granted high selectivity and anti-interference ability to the sensor. In addition, the stability of the sensor was greatly improved due to the non-accumulation of CDT nanoparticles and RB molecules in the presence of the AAS. The proposed method was able to determine TC in complex real samples with satisfactory recovery, and the obtained results were validated with standard high-performance liquid chromatography technique.

head.ce:title: Nanostructured rhodamine B/aluminosilicate extracted sugarcane bagasse modified with tobacco-derived carbon quantum dot as ratiometric fluorescence probe for determination of tetracycline

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Faculty of Chemistry, University of Mazandaran, Babolsar, Iran
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Faculty of Chemistry, University of Mazandaran, Babolsar, Iran

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Tetracycline (TC), as a widely used antibiotic, is very useful in treating bacterial infections. However, its residues in animal foodstuffs can enter the human body through the food cycle and causes severe and chronic diseases. On the other hand, due to its weak non-biodegradability, it is considered a threat to the environment. In this regard, the development of sensing methods to detect and measure TC is need of the hour. Herein, a dual-emission fluorescence sensor based on porous aluminosilicate structure (ASS) with rough surface hexagonal shape morphology and pore diameter less than 2 nm was prepared. The porous AAS was modified by post-modification method with blue carbon dots (CDT) and rhodamine B (RB) as two fluorophores to develop the ratiometric fluorescence (RF) sensor (CDT-AAS/RB). Nanostructured CDT-AAS/RB emitted two resolved peaks at 445 and 585 nm , which were dramatically quenched in the presence of TC. The RF sensor, with excellent sensitivity, was able to measure TC over the linear range of 0.001–150 μM with a limit of detection of 5.4 nM in the aqueous phosphate buffer. Moreover, the AAS component granted high selectivity and anti-interference ability to the sensor. In addition, the stability of the sensor was greatly improved due to the non-accumulation of CDT nanoparticles and RB molecules in the presence of the AAS. The proposed method was able to determine TC in complex real samples with satisfactory recovery, and the obtained results were validated with standard high-performance liquid chromatography technique.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Tetracycline (TC) is widely used in medicine, animal husbandry and aquaculture as an inexpensive, broad-spectrum and effective antibiotic in the treatment of bacterial infections [1]. This antibiotic prevents microbial protein synthesis by binding with 30S subunit of ribosome, which is widely used as the feed additive to promote animal growth [2]. Generally, a large amount of TC is excreted from the body due to its low metabolic level in organisms; therefore, it causes contamination of soil and water. On the other hand, TC antibiotic has low biodegradability in the environment and can easily enter the food cycle [3]. Long-term consumption of contaminated foods with TC residues causes irreparable damage to the liver and digestion. Also, bacterial resistance and emergence of new microbial genomes are other dangerous consequences of TC residues in environment [4]. Considering the determination of maximum residual limit of antibiotics (MRL) in food, the design of powerful and efficient analytical methods is much required. Traditional methods such as high-performance liquid chromatography with ultraviolet detector (HPLC-UV) [5], liquid chromatography-mass spectrometry (LC-MS) [6] and capillary electrophoresis [7] are very precise approaches for TC assay, but at the same time are high expensive. The time-consuming analysis, the need for experienced operators, as well as the tedious pretreatment methods, severely limit the use of these techniques in large-scale screening [8,9]. In recent years, new technologies based on fluorescence sensor have found excellent status in food safety by virtue of their quick, easy, reliable and cost-effective diagnostic approach for the detection of antibiotics.

One of the aspects of the fluorescence technique that should be taken into account is the use of suitable optical probes, which is generally made by metallic nanoclusters [10], quantum dots [11], nanosheets [12], metal–organic frameworks (MOFs) [13], and polynuclear lanthanide polymers [14]. Recently, carbon quantum dots (CQDs) as a type of carbon-based nanomaterials have attracted widespread attention in optical sensing due to their superior properties, including low toxicity, high quantum fluorescence efficiency, excellent biocompatibility, and easy surface modification [15–17]. Currently, many researchers have tried to design more simple, green, and cheap synthetic routes to obtain fluorescent CQDs. The use of biomass wastes such as lemon peel [18], coffee grounds [19], etc., for the preparation of CQDs via the bottom up strategy has been reported recently. The effective conversion of biomass wastes into CQDs can not only realize the resource utilization, but also leads to their management, reducing the harmfulness and the pressure of resource shortage. The use of biomass wastes also reduces the environmental pollution caused by their improper management. For example, in tobacco industry, low-quality leaves and many of its by-products are discarded as waste. Therefore, large amounts of tobacco waste are produced every year, which in practice are directly buried or incinerated. The preparation of CQDs from tobacco biomass waste can be considered as a green method to reduce the necessity of supplying commercial precursors such as citric acid with 3-aminophenyl boronic acid [20] and ethane-diamine [21] as carbon sources. Despite the fact that various CQDs have been synthesized and shown good performance as optical sensors, most of them have low selectivity towards specific species in real samples. In this context, it is necessary to increase the CQDs potential as photoluminescent sensors by modifying them with porous materials.

Compounds with aluminosilicate structure (ASS) such as zeolites are one of the superior candidates for loading functional materials, including metal nanoparticles, metal oxides, etc., due to their large external surface area, excellent chemical stability, electronic and mechanical properties [22–25]. These materials have presented in versatile applications including ion exchangers [26], catalysts [27], and especially effective adsorbents [28]. Since, several authors reported that ASS are efficient adsorbents with high affinity toward TC [29–32]. This is attributed to the specific sites of silanol groups on the surface of ASS, wide channels, shelves with large openings, and relatively large cations that move freely inside the holes [33]. Therefore, due to the satisfactory success of ASS in the selective adsorption of pollutants such as TC from contaminated sources, they can be used as a component for selecting and enriching the analyte in optical sensors. Bearing these in mind, ASS can be used as effective support to increase the selectivity of CQDs for fluorescence TC measurement. Considering that many efforts have been made in the direction of ASS synthesis using commercial raw materials such as tetraethyl silica, colloidal silica, and sodium silicate, and many of these materials have limitations such as unavailability and high cost. In this regard, in order to achieve economical and green synthesis, it is suggested to use agricultural wastes e.g., sugarcane bagasse (SB) that have high silica for the synthesis of ASS. On the other hand, it is noteworthy that analytical strategies, resulting from the monochromatic fluorimetric signal changes, are prone to fluorescence fluctuations due to reagent concentration, environmental factors, and instrument deviation, and thus lead to false-positive or false-negative assay results. To solve this challenge, the use of other guest fluorophores to construct ratiometric fluorescence (RF) sensors has been introduced as an efficient method for accurate analysis [33–37]. The emission intensities ratio at two wavelengths is independent of the above interference factors; therefore, RF sensors can reduce shortcomings of single-color fluorescence sensing by self-calibration caused by dual emission and achieve higher accuracy detection [38]. Nowadays, rhodamine dyes are used to make RF sensors because of their excellent spectroscopic properties, such as long absorption and emission wavelengths, high fluorescence quantum efficiency, and high photo-stability [39,40].

Inspired by the above-mentioned considerations, a dual-emission carbon dot-derived waste tobacco (CDT)-functionalized rhodamine B/ASS (CDT-AAS/RB) sensor was prepared that enabled the fluorimetric determination of TC with advantages including simplicity, speed of measurment, wide linear range, high sensitivity, and selectivity. For this aim, CDT nanoparticles with blue emission at ∼445 nm were prepared using tobacco waste and were physically embedded in porous ASS modified with RB. The CDT and RB were served as two independent emission sources in the proposed sensor. For CDT-AAS/RB, mechanism of RF detection relies on attenuation of the emission intensities to varying degrees.

The proposed sensor undergoes a concentration-dependent quenching of fluorescence intensity after exposure to TC owing to the existence of the inner filter effect (IFE). By taking relative fluorescence intensity variations, the dual-readout-output CDT-AAS/RB sensor is provided which offers more selectivity toward TC rather than the probe without ASS, indicating the desirable conditions for the selective adsorption of analyte by porous structure, which is a prerequisite for specific detection. Such methods not only prevent the waste of resources, but also solve serious environmental problems far from the requirements of sustainable environmental development and even a threat to human health.
Information related to chemicals, physical characterizations, and silica extraction from sugarcane bagasse (SISB) is provided in supporting information.

CDT nanoparticles were prepared based on a simple and economical method. In this way, firstly, tobacco leaves were washed, dried, and then turned into powder with a mortar and pestle. Tobacco leaves is a source of carbon and nitrogen that can be used for the synthesis of CD. CHNSO analysis was used for estimation of C, H, N, S, and O percent in tobacco leaves. The results showed the presence of hydrogen (3.99 %), carbon (38.11 %), nitrogen (2.36 %), oxygen (42.93 %), and sulfur (0.03 %). For the synthesis of CDT, tobacco leaves (2 g) were mixed with 1.25 mL of ethylenediamine and diluted with deionized water to a volume of 50 mL. Then, the mixture was transferred to the autoclave and heated at 220 °C for 12 h. After cooling to room temperature, CDT sample was centrifuged and filtered by a 0.22 μm membrane to remove larger particles. Finally, the brown CDT solution was stored in refrigerator at 4 °C for further usage.

For synthesis of ASS, two solutions were prepared. Extracted silica (1 g) was well dissolved in 15 mL of 4 M NaOH (Sol. 1). Simultaneously, sodium aluminate (0.54 g) was added to 15 mL of deionized water (Sol. 2). Both solutions were stirred for 1 h at room temperature on a magnetic stirrer and then Sol. 1 was added drop by drop to the Sol. 2. The mixture was stirred for 1 h at 600 rpm and then subjected to ultrasonic irradiation for 45 mi. Finally, the obtained solution was transferred to an autoclave and heated in an oven at 100 °C for 24 h. ASS powder was collected via centrifuging at 8000 rpm for 5 min.

The probe was prepared via a fast and easy procedure. Firstly, 0.1 g of ASS white powder was mixed with 10 mL of deionized water and 0.05 g of RB for 3 h on a magnetic stirrer. Then, the mixture was centrifuged, washed, and dried in an oven. In order to prepare the CDT-AAS/RB sensor, different amounts of ASS-RB composite (1, 3, 5, 7, and 9 mg) were dispersed by ultrasonic waves in 5 mL of 0.1 M phospate buffer solution (PBS), and then 0.5 mL CDT solution was added to them, separately. The mixture was stirred again and then, centrifuged to collect CDT-AAS/RB powder. The schematic procedure for preparation of ASS and CDT-AAS/RB is shown in Fig. 1 A. For comparison, the CDT-RB probe was prepared via the same procedure without addition of ASS.

The probe suspension was prepared through dissolving the CDT-AAS/RB powder (1–9 mg) in 5 mL PBS. Then, the solution (3 mL) was transferred to a quartz cell and the fluorescence emission of the probe was recorded in the presence of different concentrations of TC at an excitation wavelength of 370 nm and a 5-5 bandwidth. Evaluation of interferometry and selectivity of CDT-AAS/RB and CDT/RB were investigated in the presence of similar antibiotics and other interfering agents such as chlortetracycline (CTC), oxytetracycline (OTC), ciprofloxacin (Cip), cysteine (Cys), erythromycin (Gen), glycine (Gly), histidine (His), gentamycin (Gen), glucose (Gluc), glutamine (Glut), and leucine (Leu). The reproducibility and repeatability of CDT-AAS/RB for TC monitoring were evaluated via time-dependent fluorescence spectra during 1–30 days. The reliability of CDT-AAS/RB for determination of TC in tap water and honey samples was evaluated to verify its performance in real sample analysis.

In order to measure pHPZC, firstly, 40 mL of 0.1 M NaNO3 solution was added to ten Erlenmeyer flasks. The initial pH values (pH0) were adjusted to 3, 4, 5, 6, 7, 8, 9, 11, 12 via the addition of specific amount of NaOH (0.1 M) and HNO3 (0.1 M). After that, 5 mg of CDT-AAS/RB was added to all the solutions and flasks were placed onto the shaker for 24 h. The secondary pHs were read after the time elapsed and the plot of pH-pH0 versus pH0 was drawn. pHPZC is extracted from the plot where the pH-pH0 is equal to be zero.

For pretreatment of honey sample for analysis by flouresent sensor and HPLC technique, TC was extracted from the sample tissue based on the previously reported method [41]. Fresh honey was purchased from a local market (Babolsar, Mazandaran province, Iran) and was used to evaluate the applicability of the proposed sensor for determination of TC. Firstly, honey sample (1 g) was transferred to a series of 50 mL beakers containing 1 mL of TC standard solutions with different concentrations and 9 mL of deionized water. After that, the mixtures were centrifuged at 7000 rpm for 5 min; the upper solution was collected and filtered with a filter membrane (0.22 μm) to remove more suspended particles. For further purification, the solution was introduced to the separatory funnel, and then, 10 mL of ethyl acetate was added to it. Organic phase was collected, and this step was repeated 3 times. The solvent of the extracted solution was evaporated by rotary evaporator, and the residue was diluted with PBS (pH = 8) to a volume of 5 mL.

For the tap water, 15 mL of the samples were taken and centrifuged at 7000 rpm. Then, the upper solution was collected and diluted with 0.1 M PBS (pH = 8) with a ratio of 50:50. For fluorimetric studies, 3 mg of CDT-AAS/RB was transferred to 5 mL of each pretreated real samples and the following was performed according to section 2.4.

Firstly, XRF analysis was used to determine the weight percentage of oxides in ash of sugarcane bagasse waste, and the results are given in Table S1. As can be seen, SiO2 percent is much more than other oxides. These results confirm that sugarcane bagasse waste can be used as a cost-effective source of silica for ASS synthesis.

Fig. 1B shows the XRD patterns of different samples. The SISB with amorphous structure can dissolve in the alkaline environments to form ASS with high crystallinity. Besides, the XRD pattern for ASS shows the crystalline structure with sharp diffraction peaks. On the other hand, by matching this pattern with its standard pattern (JCPDS card no. 42–1467), the type of ASS was identified as zeolite X belongs to the Fujasite series. The dominant characteristic peaks present in the 2θ of 6.10, 10, and 11.8° are related to the (111), (220), and (311) crystal planes, respectively, and belong to the hexagonal structure with Fd-3m:2 symmetry [42]. The XRD pattern of the nanostructured CDT-AAS/RB is almost similar to the ASS diffraction pattern, which indicates that the crystalline phase does not change during the modification. However, the crystallinity of ASS was slightly reduced, which could be due to the non-crystallinity of RB. On the other hand, due to the low concentration and small size of the CDT, no corresponding peak can be observed in the XRD pattern of CDT-AAS/RB. Also, the inset shows the XRD pattern of CDT sample with a broad peak at 2θ equal to 20.76°, which corresponds to amorphous nature of carbonaceous material obtained from tobacco leaves [43].

EDS results show the presence of Si, Al, Na, and O elements in the context of ASS (Fig. S1A). In spectrum of CDT-AAS/RB (Fig. 1C), C and N appear along with all the elements defined for AAS. Fig. S1B indicates the elemental mapping of the prepared CDT-AAS/RB, which confirms the homogenous distribution of N, C, O, Si and Al in the selected region shown by FESEM. Table S2 presents the weight ratio percent (wt. %) of elements obtained via EDS analysis. From the table, Si and Al have the highest amount among the elements in the sample.

The FTIR spectra of the prepared samples were recorded in the range of 450–4000 cm−1 and are shown in Fig. S2. As can be seen in the FTIR spectrum of SISB, the absorption peaks at 500 and 800 cm−1 are related to Si–O or O–Si–O vibration bands in the silica network. The broad peak observed in the range of 1000–1200 cm−1 is due to the symmetric stretching vibrations of siloxane bonds (Si–O–Si). The bending vibration of the silanol groups is observed at 960 cm−1 [44]. The FTIR spectrum of ASS has a sharp characteristic peak at 461 cm−1, which is related to the bending vibrations of T-O bond in TO4 tetrahedral (T = Al or Si). The absorption bands at 684 and 744 cm−1 are due to the vibrations of the T-O-T symmetric double rings. On the other hand, the asymmetric T-O-T stretching vibrations cause to sharp peak with high intensity at 982 cm−1, which is observed weakly in the nanostructured CDT-AAS/RB due to the masking effect of CDT and RB molecules [45]. Besides, in the FT-IR spectrum of the CDT-AAS/RB, all the indicator peaks are almost related to ASS. The FTIR spectrum of CDT shows a sharp peak at 1641 cm−1, which is related to the CO bond. Also, the peaks between 1300 and 1480 cm−1 show the vibrational bands of C–N, and N–H groups, which indicate nitrogen doping in different forms in CDT [46]. In all samples, the absorption peak at 1700 cm−1 and the broad band at 3500 cm−1 are related to the bending and stretching vibrations of OH groups, respectively. However, the broad band at 3500 cm−1 is much wider in the case of CDT than the others, which could be due to both N–H and O–H vibrations.

Figs. S3A and B indicate the TEM images of CDT. As can be seen, the dimensions of nanoparticles are less than 10 nm. A histogram of CDT size distribution is presented in Fig. S2C. Measurements were made in eight replicates and the average diameter of the CDT was 1.81 ± 0.86 nm for a homogeneous population of particles.

Raman spectroscopy is an effective technique for identifying CDT defects. As shown in Fig. S3D, the Raman spectrum shows two distinct peaks at 1300 and 1600 cm−1, which correspond to D and G bands, respectively [47]. The D band is attributed to the disordered sp3 defects, while the G band is related to sp2 hybridized carbon atom of graphitic sheet [48]. The intensity ratio of G and D bands (ID/IG) may reflect the degree of structural disorder. The moderate ratio (0.89) shows the formation of graphitic structure without any carboneous impurities [49].

The FESEM images of the SISB are shown in Figs. S4A and B. The images show the formation of quasi-spherical nanoparticles with an average size of less than 100 nm, which are stuck together and accumulated. Fig. 2 A and B shows the formation of micrometer sized crystal of ASS with distinct edges. FESEM images of CDT-AAS/RB (Fig. 2C and D) show the presence of CDT nanoparticles on smooth surface of ASS, and no significant changes can be observed in its morphology after modification.

In TEM image of the nanostructured CDT-AAS/RB (Fig. 2E), the CDT nanoparticles (indicated by arrow) with semispherical morphology embedded into crystalline ASS are observed. The lattice network of ASS can also be seen in the images.

Brauner Emmett Teller (BET) and Barrett-Joyner-Halenda (BJH) analysis were performed to estimate the external surface area and total pores volume of ASS and CDT-AAS/RB. The N2 adsorption-desorption isotherm for ASS (Fig. S5A) revealed the type I based on classification of the International Union of Pure and Applied Chemistry (IUPAC) [50]. This type is related to microporous materials with a pore diameter of less than 2 nm. Based on the BJH result (Fig. S5B), the mean pore diameter is obtained to be 0.7 nm, which confirms the formation of structure with type I isotherm. Using the BET model the external surface area is calculated to be 729.18 m2 g−1, and the total pore volume is 0.3 cm3 g−1. After the modification of ASS with RB and CDT, both of the external surface area and the total pore volume are decreased. This phenomenon is related to the coverage of ASS surface with amorphous RB molecules; which can block some micropores. The N2 adsorption-desorption isotherm and BJH plot for CDT-AAS/RB are presented in Figs. S5C and D, respectively. In this way, the obtained BET surface area and the total pore volume are to be 588.64 m2 g−1 and 0.24 cm3 g−1, respectively. Table 1 summarizes the results provided via BET and BJH analysis. It can be deduced that CDT-AAS/RB delivered high surface and porosity, which provide the possibility for trapping TC molecules in large concentration. As known, fluorescence sensing involves the attachment of analyte molecules to the surface of the probe material, so ASS can be very helpful in this regard [51].

The optical properties of nanostructured CDT-AAS/RB are evaluated via the UV–Vis instrument. The probe has two-couple characteristic absorption bands in the wavelengths of 260 and 340 nm and the others in the range 490–560 nm. Besides, the absorption spectra of RB, ASS, and CDT as control samples were provided in Fig. 3 A. The peaks appeared at 260 and 340 nm for CDT-ASS/RB are related to the π -π* and n -π*electron transfers of CC and CO bonds in the CDT, respectively [46,52]. On the other hand, the two absorption bands in the range of 490–560 nm are assigned to RB [53]. No absorption peak is also observed for pure ASS. These findings demonstrate the successful fabrication of the ternary-component fluorimetric probe.

Fluorescence spectrum of CDT-ASS/RB displays two-resolved emission peaks at 445 and 585 nm (Fig. 3B) which are related to CDT and RB according to their pure spectra. The fluorescence intensities at 445 and 585 nm are weaker than that for its precursors, which are due to role of ASS in the suppression of guest molecules emissions. The fluorescent intensities of both RB and CDT are decreased equally after embedding into the ASS structure, which indicates that the emission behaviors of guest molecules are independent and there is no energy transfer between them [54]. Fig. 3C indicates the photographic images of CDT and CDT-ASS/RB solutions under the UV lamp (λex = 365 nm). As can be seen they have blue and pink-purple fluorescent color, respectively.

Since the fluorescence stability of CDT-AAS/RB is comprehensively affect the superior sensing performance of the probe, the fluorescence intensity ratio (F445/F585) of the CDT-AAS/RB RF sensor was examined every 5 min intervals for 3 h (Fig. 3D). Negligible instability is seen in the optical behavior of the system in the first few minutes, but it is improved over time. The stability of the optical system without ASS substrate (CDT-RB) was also studied. As seen in Fig. 3D, there is instability in the optical behavior for a longer period of time, and it can not fixed even after 20 min. It can be concluded that the ASS, as a chemical stable substrate, helps in keeping the fluorophores in place to prevent them from moving freely and sticking together.

In addition, the emission of the CDT-AAS/RB system decreased to a negligible amount after 40 days of storage in the refrigerator (Fig. S6). The amount of this reduction is about 2 % of the initial emission. These findings dedicate the formation of photo-stable ratiometric sensor with repeatable results for TC assay.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Validation of the sensing strategy for TC determination
ce:section.ce:para: The design of RF sensor based on non-emissive ASS was performed through the encapsulating of two chromophores including carbon dots and RB dye into its frameworks [38]. The fluorescence spectra of CDT-ASS/RB in the absence and presence of 100 μM of TC in 0.1 M PBS is presented in Fig. 4 A. As can be seen, the intensities of fluorescence emissions at two different wavelength are decreased with different degrees. Moreover, a magenta-to-red color change for CDT-ASS/RB sensor can be observed upon the addition of TC via naked eyes (Inset of Fig. 4A). In addition, the pink-purple color of the probe under UV lamp (λex = 365 nm) disappeared in the presence of 150 μM of TC (Fig. S7). As can be seen from the XRD patterns, the structure of the probe has not undergone significant changes after treatment with TC, and the characteristic peaks of the probe are still present (Fig. 4B). Therefore, the quenching is not due to the destruction of the probe structure.

Two common mechanisms involved in quenching process are fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET) and IFE. The absorption spectrum of TC as well as the excitation and emission spectra of the probe are presented in Fig. 4C. In absorption spectrum of TC, two peaks are observed at 265 and 350 nm which has no overlap with the emission spectrum of the probe, therefore FRET has no contribution in the mechanism of the process. On the other hand, the excitation spectrum of the probe has a very good overlap with the absorption spectrum of TC, which suggests the IFE mechanism involved in the quenching [55].

In order to find more robust evidence to confirm or reject FRET and IFE, the emission lifetimes of the probe without and after treatment with TC were recorded (Fig. 4D). The average time for fluorescent emission of the probe was measured to be 2.20 ns and, after the addition of TC, it reached to 2.17 ns. Since the fluorescence lifetime of the probe remained almost constant after addition of TC, IFE is suggested as an effective mechanism in the measurement process [56].

The quenching degree of two-resolved peaks in the presence of TC (100 μM) is evaluated with time (Fig. S8A). It can be seen that at the initial incubation time (0–1 min), the F445/F585 ratios decreases rapidly, and with further increasing time, F445/F585 does not decrease significantly. This indicates that the sensor with a high external surface area can provide effective and large detection sites for the adsorption of TC at initial times assay and will be occupied in higher incubation times [43]. In order to provide a sensitive fluorescent assay of TC, other conditions that may affect the performance of probe are studied.

The analytical signal was evaluated in different weight (mg): volume (mL) ratios of ASS/RB: CDT in the presence of TC (100 μM). As seen in Fig. S8B, the proposed sensor showed the highest measurement sensitivity in the ratio of 0.6.

The effect of pH on the sensing performance of sensor is shown in Fig. S8C. In the presence of TC, the fluorescence ratio intensity is much more sensitive to pH changes. While the F445/F585 ratios dramatically decreases until pH = 7, it increases substantially at higher pH. Therefore, pH = 7 was selected as the optimum value. TC is known as an ion-pair compound, which will be cationic at a pH less than 3.3 and anionic in pH higher than 7.7 [57]. In pH range of 3.3 to 7.7, zwitter ions are formed [58,59]. On the other hand, the surface charge of the probe is an important factor on the sensing efficiency of the sensor at various pH. pHPZC indicates at which pH the electric charge of the probe surface is neutral. On account of this, the surface of the probe becomes negatively and positively charges in higher and lower pH than pHPZC, respectively.

Fig. S8D shows the graph of changes of pH-pH0 vs. pH0. According to the figure, the pHPZC of the CDT-AAS/RB is equal to 6. Since the optimum value of F445/F585 is obtained at pH = 7, at this pH, the TC is in its neutral form, while the surface charge of CDT-ASS/RB is negative, the occurrence of the electrostatic interaction between the CDT-AAS/RB and TC and also complexion may be involved [57,58]. As can be seen in Fig. S8C, when the pHsis lower than the optimal pH, the sensing performance of probe decreases because the more positive charges are formed on the surface of CDT-ASS/RB and the electrostatic repulsion hinders the adsorption of TC, and as a result, the selective detection prerequisite is not provided. At higher pH, the electrostatic repulsion force between TC and negative surface charges of CDT-ASS/RB leads to decrease in sensing performance [60]. On account of these, the justification of the different sensitivity for the proposed sensor in different pHs can be expressed.

According to these results, the best fluorescence response of the sensor can be obtained at the optimal pH equal to 7, the weight-volume ratio of ASS/RB: CDT equal to 0.6, and the response time of 1min.

The performance of the fluorescence sensor was investigated in the presence of different concentrations of TC under optimal parameters. As seen in Fig. 5 A, the fluorescence intensities at 445 and 585 nm gradually turns off with increasing TC concentration and there is a good linear correlation between F445/F585 vs. concentrations in the range of 0.001–150 μM (Fig. 5B). LOD and LOQ were calculated to be 5.4 nM and 16.2 nM based on 3.3 and 10 times the signal-to-noise ratio (Eq. LOD = 3.3 σ/k and LOQ = 10 σ/k, where σ represents the standard deviation of the blank solution and k is the slope of the calibration curve). Fig. 5C shows the photographic images of CDT-ASS/RB emission upon successive addition of TC under UV lamp (λex = 365 nm).

Table 2 shows the efficiency of the proposed sensor, including the parameters of LOD, sensitivity and linear concentration range for TC measurment [9,55,56,61–70]. As can be seen, the results are comparable with that reported by other researchers and confirm the good performance of the proposed sensor. The CDT-AAS/RB sensor provides LOD in the nM range, which can be used to determine TC at low concentrations.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Selectivity, reproducibility, and reusability analysis of CDT-AAS/RB probe
ce:section.ce:para: The main highlight of this work is the use of porous ASS as a firm and stable substrate for the selective absorption of TC and its interactions with fluorophores to produce signals. In this regard, the behavior of the sensor was investigated for detection of amino acids, ions and other antibiotics similar to TC (CTC and OTC), which all contain tetraphenyl and beta-diketone. As can be inferred from the obtained results (Fig. 5D), the changes of F445/F585 in the presence of CTC and OTC are more noticeable compared to other antibiotics and molecules. However, they still present lower degree of quenching than TC. The sensing ability of CDT-RB in the selective detection of TC was also investigated. As seen in Fig. 5D, this sensor has no selectivity toward TC. The findings indicate the vital role of ASS in selective sensing of TC by CDT-AAS/RB.

During sensing process at pH = 7, TC is in its zwitterion form and easily diffused to the CDT-AAS/RB surface because of the small repulsion force between TC and negatively charged probe. The fluorescence intensity of CDT-AAS/RB quenches in the presence of TC through the IFE process. The selective sensing of CDT-AAS/RB can be promoted by the vital role of the AAS component in two ways: 1) ASS forms complex with TC due to the mobile non-body ions in its structure [29]. As known, ASS compounds have an inherent negative charge, which requires a cation to neutralize the lattice charge. The cations, mainly belonging to alkali or alkaline earth metals, are moved freely into ASS structure [71]. 2) It also acts as an electron donor and provide π-π stacking interaction with aromatic structure of TC, which is an electron acceptor [72,73]. These reasons cause to promote the trapping of TC onto the surface of CDT-AAS/RB.

The studies on the anti-interference ability of the CDT-ASS/RB for TC show that the fluorescence response with dual-emission in the presence of antibiotic has not affected by interferers, even when their concentrations are much higher than TC (Fig. 5E). The sensor can present excellent anti-interference ability for TC measurement in complex environments.

The reproducibility of the system was also investigated by making seven similiar probes according to the method mentioned in section 2.3 (Fig. 6 A). The negligible difference in the response of the probes in the presence of 100 μM TC was clarified by low RSD = 3.4, demonstrating the good reproducibility of CDT-AAS/RB toward TC measurement.

The reusability is another major virtue for the sensing capability (Fig. 6B). The probe suspended in 0.1 M PBS was centrifuged and washed after performing the measurement tests. The solid powder was immersed in ethanol for 2 h and then dried in an oven. Fig. 6B compares the performance of freshly prepared and recycled probe from 1 to 6 th usage in the presence of TC (100 μM). The results of investigations for six consecutive reuse cycles of the sensor indicate a loss of 10 % in the response toward TC.

In order to assess the repeatability of the probe, the fluorescence response of CDT-AAS/RB in the presence of TC (100 μM) was recorded at first day and after storage for 30 days (Fig. 6C). The results confirm the good repeatability of the sensor which is related to the high precision of the suggested method.

The sensing applicability of the probe for TC in honey and tap water was evaluated, and the results are presented in Table 3 . The pretreatment of spiked real samples with the final concentration of 0.1, 1, 50, 100 μM of TC was performed by the method explained in section 2.6, and the amount of recovery was calculated according to Recovery = Cfound/Cadded; where Cfound and Cadded denoted to detected and spiked concentration of TC, respectively. As can be seen, the recovery values obtained for the proposed method are within the acceptable range of 95–105 %, and the RSD values are less than 4. The results were compared with HPLC as a standard technique and a very good agreement was obtained for two methods. These results prove the accuracy and precision of the proposed method for detecting TC in real samples.

In this work, a dual-emission RF sensor with high selectivity and sensitivity based on a mineral compound (ASS) has been developed for determination of TC. The synthesized ASS was modified with CDT and RB fluorophores through the post-synthetic process and characterized by instrumental analysis. The electron transfer-induced IFE sensing mechanism causes excellent accuracy and sensitivity of the RF probe to TC within a linear range of 0.001–150 μM and a LOD of 5.4 nM. This sensor well detects TC in the presence of similar antibiotics such as CTC and OTC. Also, the anti-interference tests in the presence of other antibiotics and some interferences molecules and ions showed the greater tendency of the sensor to adsorb and detect TC in comparision to other species. The CDT-RB sensor showed lower optical stability and selectivity than nanostructured CDT-AAS/RB. These results show that ASS plays a crucial role in fixing the CDTs and RB molecules on their sites and interactions with TC due to the presence of non-body ions in its structure. The proposed method was used for determination of TC in tap water and honey samples and the recovery percentage were estimated between 95 and 105. These values were compared with the recovery percentage obtained by the HPLC method, and a good agreement was observed between the two methods. These results motivate the development of RF probes for the detection of antibiotics with greatly improved selectivity.
